---
title: "Wooldridge Introductory Econometrics -- Ch. 8: Heteroskedasticity (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Jeffrey M. Wooldridge
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

**Source.** Wooldridge, J. M., *Introductory Econometrics: A Modern Approach*
(Cengage Learning). Chapter 8: *Heteroskedasticity*. The Stata commands and
their published output are reproduced verbatim in each `#= … =#` block; the
Julia translation uses the [StatEcon](https://github.com/ElvisCasco/StatEcon)
package.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

# Artifacts this notebook produces go to `output/`.
const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)
```

Datasets are loaded **by name** from the bundle shipped with StatEcon. Bare
names resolve when unique; `mroz` ships in two folders, so it is qualified
with `wooldridge_intro/`.

# Example 8.1: Log Wage Equation with Heteroscedasticity-Robust Standard Errors

```{julia}
#=
use "data/wage1.dta", clear
gen single=(~married)
gen male=(~female)
gen marrmale=male*married
gen marrfem=female*married
gen singfem=single*female
reg lwage marrmale marrfem singfem educ exper expersq tenure tenursq, robust

Linear regression                               Number of obs     =        526
                                                F(8, 517)         =      51.70
                                                Prob > F          =     0.0000
                                                R-squared         =     0.4609
                                                Root MSE          =     .39329
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
             |               Robust
       lwage | Coefficient  std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
    marrmale |   .2126757   .0571419     3.72   0.000     .1004168    .3249346
     marrfem |  -.1982676     .05877    -3.37   0.001     -.313725   -.0828102
     singfem |  -.1103502   .0571163    -1.93   0.054    -.2225587    .0018583
        educ |   .0789103   .0074147    10.64   0.000     .0643437    .0934769
       exper |   .0268006   .0051391     5.22   0.000     .0167044    .0368967
     expersq |  -.0005352   .0001063    -5.03   0.000    -.0007442   -.0003263
      tenure |   .0290875   .0069409     4.19   0.000     .0154516    .0427234
     tenursq |  -.0005331   .0002437    -2.19   0.029    -.0010119   -.0000544
       _cons |   .3213781    .109469     2.94   0.003     .1063193    .5364368
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
wage1 = DataFrame(dataset("wage1"))
wage1.single   = ifelse.(wage1.married .== 0, 1.0, 0.0)
wage1.male     = ifelse.(wage1.female  .== 0, 1.0, 0.0)
wage1.marrmale = wage1.male   .* wage1.married
wage1.marrfem  = wage1.female .* wage1.married
wage1.singfem  = wage1.single .* wage1.female

m81r = stata_regress(wage1; y = :lwage,
    x = [:marrmale, :marrfem, :singfem, :educ, :exper, :expersq, :tenure, :tenursq],
    vce = :robust)
```

```{julia}
#=
reg lwage marrmale marrfem singfem educ exper expersq tenure tenursq

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
       lwage | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
    marrmale |   .2126757   .0553572     3.84   0.000      .103923    .3214284
     marrfem |  -.1982676   .0578355    -3.43   0.001     -.311889   -.0846462
     singfem |  -.1103502   .0557421    -1.98   0.048     -.219859   -.0008414
        educ |   .0789103   .0066945    11.79   0.000     .0657585     .092062
       exper |   .0268006   .0052428     5.11   0.000     .0165007    .0371005
     expersq |  -.0005352   .0001104    -4.85   0.000    -.0007522   -.0003183
      tenure |   .0290875    .006762     4.30   0.000     .0158031    .0423719
     tenursq |  -.0005331   .0002312    -2.31   0.022    -.0009874   -.0000789
       _cons |   .3213781    .100009     3.21   0.001     .1249041    .5178521
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
m81 = stata_regress(wage1; y = :lwage,
    x = [:marrmale, :marrfem, :singfem, :educ, :exper, :expersq, :tenure, :tenursq])
```

The point estimates are identical between the two fits; only the standard
errors change. StatEcon's `vce = :robust` reproduces Stata's HC1 robust
standard errors digit for digit (e.g. `marrmale` SE `.0571419` robust vs
`.0553572` classical), as does the classical fit.

# Example 8.2: Heteroscedasticity-Robust F Statistics

```{julia}
#=
use "data/gpa3.dta", clear
reg cumgpa sat hsperc tothrs female black white if term==2, robust

Linear regression                               Number of obs     =        366
                                                F(6, 359)         =      39.30
                                                Prob > F          =     0.0000
                                                R-squared         =     0.4006
                                                Root MSE          =     .46929
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
             |               Robust
      cumgpa | Coefficient  std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
         sat |   .0011407   .0001915     5.96   0.000     .0007641    .0015174
      hsperc |  -.0085664   .0014179    -6.04   0.000    -.0113548   -.0057779
      tothrs |    .002504   .0007406     3.38   0.001     .0010475    .0039605
      female |   .3034333   .0591378     5.13   0.000     .1871332    .4197334
       black |  -.1282837   .1192413    -1.08   0.283    -.3627829    .1062155
       white |  -.0587217    .111392    -0.53   0.598    -.2777846    .1603411
       _cons |   1.470065   .2206802     6.66   0.000     1.036076    1.904053
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
gpa3  = DataFrame(dataset("gpa3"))
gpa3t = gpa3[gpa3.term .== 2, :]     # the "if term==2" sample, N = 366

m82r = stata_regress(gpa3t; y = :cumgpa,
    x = [:sat, :hsperc, :tothrs, :female, :black, :white], vce = :robust)
```

```{julia}
#=
reg cumgpa sat hsperc tothrs female black white if term==2

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      cumgpa | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
         sat |   .0011407   .0001786     6.39   0.000     .0007896    .0014919
      hsperc |  -.0085664   .0012404    -6.91   0.000    -.0110058    -.006127
      tothrs |    .002504    .000731     3.43   0.001     .0010664    .0039415
      female |   .3034333   .0590203     5.14   0.000     .1873643    .4195023
       black |  -.1282837   .1473701    -0.87   0.385    -.4181009    .1615335
       white |  -.0587217   .1409896    -0.42   0.677    -.3359909    .2185475
       _cons |   1.470065   .2298031     6.40   0.000     1.018135    1.921994
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
m82 = stata_regress(gpa3t; y = :cumgpa,
    x = [:sat, :hsperc, :tothrs, :female, :black, :white])
```

The heteroskedasticity-robust joint test of `black` and `white` — the point of
this example — uses the robust variance-covariance matrix of the robust fit:

```{julia}
#=  test black white   (after the robust regression)  =#
stata_test(m82r, [:black, :white])
```

Both coefficient tables match Stata exactly. `stata_test` on the robust model
returns a heteroskedasticity-robust Wald F; there is no published Stata target
for this particular joint test, so it is shown as an illustration of the robust
F machinery the example is named for.

# Example 8.3: Heteroskedasticity-Robust LM Statistic

```{julia}
#=
use "data/crime1.dta", clear
gen avgsensq=avgsen*avgsen
reg narr86 pcnv avgsen avgsensq ptime86 qemp86 inc86 black hispan, robust

Linear regression                               Number of obs     =      2,725
                                                F(8, 2716)        =      29.84
                                                Prob > F          =     0.0000
                                                R-squared         =     0.0728
                                                Root MSE          =     .82843
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
             |               Robust
      narr86 | Coefficient  std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
        pcnv |  -.1355954   .0336218    -4.03   0.000    -.2015223   -.0696685
      avgsen |   .0178411   .0101233     1.76   0.078    -.0020091    .0376913
    avgsensq |  -.0005163   .0002077    -2.49   0.013    -.0009236   -.0001091
     ptime86 |    -.03936   .0062236    -6.32   0.000    -.0515634   -.0271566
      qemp86 |  -.0505072   .0142015    -3.56   0.000     -.078354   -.0226603
       inc86 |  -.0014797   .0002295    -6.45   0.000    -.0019297   -.0010296
       black |   .3246024   .0585135     5.55   0.000     .2098669     .439338
      hispan |     .19338   .0402983     4.80   0.000     .1143616    .2723985
       _cons |   .5670128   .0402756    14.08   0.000     .4880389    .6459867
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
crime1 = DataFrame(dataset("crime1"))
crime1.avgsensq = crime1.avgsen .^ 2

m83r = stata_regress(crime1; y = :narr86,
    x = [:pcnv, :avgsen, :avgsensq, :ptime86, :qemp86, :inc86, :black, :hispan],
    vce = :robust)
```

Turning point for `avgsen`:

```{julia}
#=  di _b[avgsen]/(2*_b[avgsensq])   ->  -17.276862  =#
b = coef(m83r); nm = coefnames(m83r)
tp = b[findfirst(==("avgsen"), nm)] / (2 * b[findfirst(==("avgsensq"), nm)])
@printf("Turning point for avgsen = %.6f\n", tp)
```

```{julia}
#=
reg narr86 pcnv ptime86 qemp86 inc86 black hispan

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      narr86 | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
        pcnv |  -.1322784   .0403406    -3.28   0.001    -.2113797    -.053177
     ptime86 |  -.0377953    .008497    -4.45   0.000    -.0544566    -.021134
      qemp86 |  -.0509814   .0144359    -3.53   0.000    -.0792878    -.022675
       inc86 |    -.00149   .0003404    -4.38   0.000    -.0021575   -.0008224
       black |   .3296885   .0451778     7.30   0.000     .2411022    .4182748
      hispan |   .1954509   .0396929     4.92   0.000     .1176195    .2732823
       _cons |   .5703344   .0360073    15.84   0.000       .49973    .6409388
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
m83 = stata_regress(crime1; y = :narr86,
    x = [:pcnv, :ptime86, :qemp86, :inc86, :black, :hispan])
```

Auxiliary regressions for the **robust** LM statistic (Wooldridge's procedure:
residualize `avgsen` and `avgsensq` on the other regressors, form the products
with the restricted residuals, and regress a column of ones on those products
without a constant; the robust LM is `N − SSR`):

```{julia}
#=
predict ubar1, resid
qui reg avgsen pcnv ptime86 qemp86 inc86 black hispan
predict r1, r
qui reg avgsensq pcnv ptime86 qemp86 inc86 black hispan
predict r2, r
qui gen ur1 = ubar1*r1
qui gen ur2 = ubar1*r2
gen iota = 1
qui reg iota ur1 ur2, noconstant
scalar hetlm = e(N)-e(rss)
scalar pval = chi2tail(2,hetlm)
display "Robust LM statistic : " %6.3f hetlm
display "Under H0, distrib Chi2(2), p-value: " %5.3f pval

Robust LM statistic :  3.997
Under H0, distrib Chi2(2), p-value: 0.136
=#
crime1.ubar1 = crime1.narr86 .- FixedEffectModels.predict(m83, crime1)
r1m = reg(crime1, @formula(avgsen   ~ pcnv + ptime86 + qemp86 + inc86 + black + hispan))
r2m = reg(crime1, @formula(avgsensq ~ pcnv + ptime86 + qemp86 + inc86 + black + hispan))
crime1.ur1 = crime1.ubar1 .* (crime1.avgsen   .- FixedEffectModels.predict(r1m, crime1))
crime1.ur2 = crime1.ubar1 .* (crime1.avgsensq .- FixedEffectModels.predict(r2m, crime1))

let n = nrow(crime1)
    Z   = hcat(crime1.ur1, crime1.ur2)
    bb  = Z \ ones(n)
    ssr = sum(abs2, ones(n) .- Z * bb)
    hetlm = n - ssr
    pval  = 1 - cdf(Chisq(2), hetlm)
    @printf("Robust LM statistic : %6.3f\n", hetlm)
    @printf("Under H0, distrib Chi2(2), p-value: %5.3f\n", pval)
end
```

Non-robust LM statistic (`N·R²` of the restricted residuals on the full
regressor set):

```{julia}
#=
qui reg narr86 pcnv ptime86 qemp86 inc86 black hispan
predict ubar2, resid
qui reg ubar2 pcnv avgsen avgsensq ptime86 qemp86 inc86 black hispan
scalar lm1 = e(N)*e(r2)
display "LM statistic : " %6.3f lm1

LM statistic :  3.463
=#
crime1.ubar2 = crime1.narr86 .- FixedEffectModels.predict(m83, crime1)
aux = reg(crime1, @formula(ubar2 ~ pcnv + avgsen + avgsensq + ptime86 + qemp86 + inc86 + black + hispan))
@printf("LM statistic : %6.3f\n", nobs(aux) * r2(aux))
```

The robust LM statistic (`3.997`, p `0.136`) and the non-robust LM statistic
(`3.463`) both reproduce Stata to the printed precision, as do the two
coefficient tables and the turning point `−17.276862`.

# Example 8.4: Heteroscedasticity in Housing Price Equation

Stata uses the user-written `whitetst` (White's general test). StatEcon's
`estat_imtest` returns the Cameron–Trivedi decomposition of the
information-matrix test, whose **Heteroskedasticity** component is exactly
White's general statistic.

```{julia}
#=
use "data/hprice1.dta", clear
reg price lotsize sqrft bdrms

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
       price | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     lotsize |   .0020677   .0006421     3.22   0.002     .0007908    .0033446
       sqrft |   .1227782   .0132374     9.28   0.000     .0964541    .1491022
       bdrms |   13.85252   9.010145     1.54   0.128    -4.065141    31.77018
       _cons |  -21.77031   29.47504    -0.74   0.462    -80.38466    36.84405
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
hprice1 = DataFrame(dataset("hprice1"))
m84 = stata_regress(hprice1; y = :price, x = [:lotsize, :sqrft, :bdrms])
```

```{julia}
#=  whitetst  ->  White's general test statistic : 33.73166  Chi-sq(9)  P-value = 1.0e-04  =#
r84 = estat_imtest(m84, hprice1)
@printf("White's general (Het component): %.5f  Chi-sq(%d)  p = %.4g\n",
        r84.H.chi2, r84.H.df, r84.H.p)
```

```{julia}
#=
reg lprice llotsize lsqrft bdrms

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      lprice | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
    llotsize |   .1679667   .0382812     4.39   0.000     .0918404     .244093
      lsqrft |   .7002324   .0928652     7.54   0.000     .5155597    .8849051
       bdrms |   .0369584   .0275313     1.34   0.183    -.0177906    .0917074
       _cons |  -1.297042   .6512836    -1.99   0.050    -2.592191    -.001893
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
hprice1.lprice   = log.(hprice1.price)
hprice1.llotsize = log.(hprice1.lotsize)
hprice1.lsqrft   = log.(hprice1.sqrft)
m84b = stata_regress(hprice1; y = :lprice, x = [:llotsize, :lsqrft, :bdrms])
```

```{julia}
#=  whitetst  ->  White's general test statistic : 9.549449  Chi-sq(9)  P-value = .3882  =#
r84b = estat_imtest(m84b, hprice1)
@printf("White's general (Het component): %.5f  Chi-sq(%d)  p = %.4f\n",
        r84b.H.chi2, r84b.H.df, r84b.H.p)
```

The level equation gives White's statistic `33.717` (Stata `33.73166`) and the
log equation `9.549` (Stata `9.549449`), both on 9 df — matching to the printed
precision. Taking logs of `price` and `lotsize` removes the strong evidence of
heteroskedasticity, exactly as Wooldridge reports.

# Example 8.5: Special Form of the White Test in the Log Housing Price Equation

The Stata script re-runs `whitetst` (general form) on the log housing-price
equation, so the published number is again White's general statistic.

```{julia}
#=
use "data/hprice1.dta", clear
qui reg lprice llotsize lsqrft bdrms
whitetst

White's general test statistic :  9.549449  Chi-sq( 9)  P-value =  .3882
=#
r85 = estat_imtest(m84b, hprice1)
@printf("White's general (Het component): %.5f  Chi-sq(%d)  p = %.4f\n",
        r85.H.chi2, r85.H.df, r85.H.p)
```

For reference, Wooldridge's *special form* of the White test regresses `û²` on
the fitted values `ŷ` and `ŷ²` (an LM test with 2 df):

```{julia}
hprice1.uhat2 = (hprice1.lprice .- FixedEffectModels.predict(m84b, hprice1)) .^ 2
hprice1.yhat  = FixedEffectModels.predict(m84b, hprice1)
hprice1.yhat2 = hprice1.yhat .^ 2
sp = reg(hprice1, @formula(uhat2 ~ yhat + yhat2))
@printf("Special-form LM = N*R2 = %.3f  Chi-sq(2)  p = %.4f\n",
        nobs(sp) * r2(sp), 1 - cdf(Chisq(2), nobs(sp) * r2(sp)))
```

The general-form statistic reproduces Stata exactly (`9.549`). The special form
(2 df) is a different, lower-powered test and is shown only for context.

# Example 8.6: Family Saving Equation

Stata's analytic weights `[aw = 1/inc]` are ordinary WLS. StatEcon's
`stata_regress` accepts a `weights =` column (via FixedEffectModels), which
reproduces Stata's `aweight` estimates and standard errors exactly.

```{julia}
#=
use "data/saving.dta", clear
reg sav inc

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
         sav | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
         inc |   .1466283   .0575488     2.55   0.012     .0324247     .260832
       _cons |   124.8424   655.3931     0.19   0.849    -1175.764    1425.449
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
saving = DataFrame(dataset("saving"))
m86a = stata_regress(saving; y = :sav, x = [:inc])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg sav inc [aw = 1/inc]

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
         sav | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
         inc |   .1717555   .0568128     3.02   0.003     .0590124    .2844986
       _cons |  -124.9528   480.8606    -0.26   0.796    -1079.205    829.2994
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
saving.w = 1.0 ./ saving.inc
m86b = stata_regress(saving, :sav, [:inc]; weights = :w)
```

```{julia}
#=
reg sav inc size educ age black

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
         sav | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
         inc |    .109455   .0714317     1.53   0.129    -.0323742    .2512842
        size |   67.66119   222.9642     0.30   0.762    -375.0395    510.3619
        educ |   151.8235   117.2487     1.29   0.199    -80.97646    384.6235
         age |   .2857217   50.03108     0.01   0.995    -99.05217    99.62361
       black |   518.3934   1308.063     0.40   0.693    -2078.796    3115.583
       _cons |  -1605.416   2830.707    -0.57   0.572    -7225.851    4015.019
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
m86c = stata_regress(saving; y = :sav, x = [:inc, :size, :educ, :age, :black])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg sav inc size educ age black [aw = 1/inc]

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
         sav | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
         inc |   .1005179   .0772511     1.30   0.196     -.052866    .2539018
        size |  -6.868501   168.4327    -0.04   0.968    -341.2956    327.5586
        educ |   139.4802   100.5362     1.39   0.169    -60.13681    339.0972
         age |   21.74721   41.30598     0.53   0.600    -60.26678    103.7612
       black |   137.2842   844.5941     0.16   0.871    -1539.677    1814.246
       _cons |  -1854.814   2351.797    -0.79   0.432    -6524.362    2814.734
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
m86d = stata_regress(saving, :sav, [:inc, :size, :educ, :age, :black]; weights = :w)
```

All four fits match Stata to the printed precision. The `weights = :w` column
`w = 1/inc` reproduces Stata's `[aw = 1/inc]` coefficients, standard errors and
R² exactly (WLS = OLS on data scaled by `1/√inc`).

# Example 8.7: Demand for Cigarettes

```{julia}
#=
use "data/smoke.dta", clear
reg cigs lincome lcigpric educ age agesq restaurn

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        cigs | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     lincome |   .8802682   .7277832     1.21   0.227     -.548322    2.308858
    lcigpric |  -.7508586   5.773343    -0.13   0.897    -12.08355    10.58183
        educ |  -.5014982   .1670772    -3.00   0.003    -.8294597   -.1735368
         age |   .7706936   .1601223     4.81   0.000      .456384    1.085003
       agesq |  -.0090228    .001743    -5.18   0.000    -.0124443   -.0056013
    restaurn |  -2.825085   1.111794    -2.54   0.011    -5.007462   -.6427078
       _cons |  -3.639841   24.07866    -0.15   0.880    -50.90466    43.62497
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
smoke = DataFrame(dataset("smoke"))
m87 = stata_regress(smoke; y = :cigs,
    x = [:lincome, :lcigpric, :educ, :age, :agesq, :restaurn])
```

```{julia}
#=  display _b[lincome]*10/100   ->  .08802682   (change in cigs if income +10%)
    display _b[age]/2/_b[agesq]  ->  -42.708116  (turnover point for age)      =#
b = coef(m87); nm = coefnames(m87)
@printf("Change in cigs if income +10%%: %.8f\n", b[findfirst(==("lincome"), nm)] * 10 / 100)
@printf("Turnover point for age:        %.6f\n",
        b[findfirst(==("age"), nm)] / 2 / b[findfirst(==("agesq"), nm)])
```

```{julia}
#=  whitetst  ->  White's general test statistic : 52.17244  Chi-sq(25)  P-value = .0011  =#
r87 = estat_imtest(m87, smoke)
@printf("White's general (Het component): %.5f  Chi-sq(%d)  p = %.4f\n",
        r87.H.chi2, r87.H.df, r87.H.p)
```

Weighted least squares with the estimated variance function (regress
`log(û²)` on the regressors, take `ĥ = exp(fitted)`, then WLS with weights
`1/ĥ`):

```{julia}
#=
gen lubar=log(cigs*cigs)
qui reg lubar lincome lcigpric educ age agesq restaurn
predict cigsh, xb
gen cigse = exp(cigsh)
reg cigs lincome lcigpric educ age agesq restaurn [aw=1/cigse]

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        cigs | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     lincome |    1.12369   .5276854     2.13   0.034     .0878786    2.159501
    lcigpric |  -4.986817   5.049478    -0.99   0.324    -14.89861    4.924974
        educ |  -.3886421   .1554094    -2.50   0.013    -.6937005   -.0835838
         age |   .6390876   .1206064     5.30   0.000     .4023453    .8758299
       agesq |   -.007384   .0012624    -5.85   0.000    -.0098621   -.0049059
    restaurn |  -3.250421   .9610555    -3.38   0.001    -5.136909   -1.363932
       _cons |   12.16875   20.47917     0.59   0.553     -28.0305    52.36801
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
smoke.lubar = ifelse.(smoke.cigs .> 0, log.(smoke.cigs .^ 2), missing)
aux87 = reg(smoke[.!ismissing.(smoke.lubar), :],
            @formula(lubar ~ lincome + lcigpric + educ + age + agesq + restaurn))
smoke.cigse = exp.(FixedEffectModels.predict(aux87, smoke))
smoke.wcig  = 1.0 ./ smoke.cigse
m87w = stata_regress(smoke, :cigs,
    [:lincome, :lcigpric, :educ, :age, :agesq, :restaurn]; weights = :wcig)
```

The OLS table, both scalar displays (`.08802682` and `−42.708116`), and the WLS
table all reproduce Stata exactly. **White's test differs in one detail:** the
statistic matches (`52.172`), but StatEcon reports **26 df** where Stata's
`whitetst` reports **25 df**. Because `agesq` (= `age²`) is already a regressor,
`whitetst` drops the redundant `age×age` column from the auxiliary regression;
`estat_imtest`'s collinearity screen does not catch this particular redundancy,
so it keeps the near-collinear column. The statistic is essentially unchanged
(the extra column contributes nothing), but the extra degree of freedom nudges
the p-value from `.0011` to `.0017`.

# Example 8.8: Labor Force Participation of Married Women

```{julia}
#=
use "data/mroz.dta", clear
reg inlf nwifeinc educ exper expersq age kidslt6 kidsge6

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        inlf | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
    nwifeinc |  -.0034052   .0014485    -2.35   0.019    -.0062488   -.0005616
        educ |   .0379953    .007376     5.15   0.000      .023515    .0524756
       exper |   .0394924   .0056727     6.96   0.000     .0283561    .0506287
     expersq |  -.0005963   .0001848    -3.23   0.001    -.0009591   -.0002335
         age |  -.0160908   .0024847    -6.48   0.000    -.0209686    -.011213
     kidslt6 |  -.2618105   .0335058    -7.81   0.000    -.3275875   -.1960335
     kidsge6 |   .0130122    .013196     0.99   0.324    -.0128935    .0389179
       _cons |   .5855192    .154178     3.80   0.000     .2828442    .8881943
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
mroz = DataFrame(dataset("wooldridge_intro/mroz"))
m88 = stata_regress(mroz; y = :inlf,
    x = [:nwifeinc, :educ, :exper, :expersq, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg inlf nwifeinc educ exper expersq age kidslt6 kidsge6, robust

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
             |               Robust
        inlf | Coefficient  std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
    nwifeinc |  -.0034052   .0015249    -2.23   0.026    -.0063988   -.0004115
        educ |   .0379953    .007266     5.23   0.000      .023731    .0522596
       exper |   .0394924     .00581     6.80   0.000     .0280864    .0508983
     expersq |  -.0005963     .00019    -3.14   0.002    -.0009693   -.0002233
         age |  -.0160908    .002399    -6.71   0.000    -.0208004   -.0113812
     kidslt6 |  -.2618105   .0317832    -8.24   0.000    -.3242058   -.1994152
     kidsge6 |   .0130122   .0135329     0.96   0.337     -.013555    .0395795
       _cons |   .5855192   .1522599     3.85   0.000     .2866098    .8844287
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
m88r = stata_regress(mroz; y = :inlf,
    x = [:nwifeinc, :educ, :exper, :expersq, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6],
    vce = :robust)
```

This linear probability model is inherently heteroskedastic. The classical and
robust fits both match Stata exactly; the robust standard errors move only
slightly (e.g. `nwifeinc` SE `.0014485` → `.0015249`).

# Example 8.9: Determinants of Personal Computer Ownership

```{julia}
#=
use "data/gpa1.dta", clear
gen parcoll = (mothcoll | fathcoll)
reg PC hsGPA ACT parcoll

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
          PC | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       hsGPA |   .0653943   .1372576     0.48   0.635    -.2060231    .3368118
         ACT |   .0005645   .0154967     0.04   0.971    -.0300792    .0312082
     parcoll |   .2210541    .092957     2.38   0.019      .037238    .4048702
       _cons |  -.0004322   .4905358    -0.00   0.999     -.970433    .9695686
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
gpa1 = DataFrame(dataset("gpa1"))
gpa1.parcoll = Float64.((gpa1.mothcoll .== 1) .| (gpa1.fathcoll .== 1))
m89 = stata_regress(gpa1; y = :PC, x = [:hsGPA, :ACT, :parcoll])
```

```{julia}
#=
predict phat
gen h=phat*(1-phat)
reg PC hsGPA ACT parcoll [aw=1/h]

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
          PC | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       hsGPA |   .0327029   .1298817     0.25   0.802    -.2241292     .289535
         ACT |    .004272   .0154527     0.28   0.783    -.0262847    .0348286
     parcoll |   .2151862   .0862918     2.49   0.014       .04455    .3858224
       _cons |   .0262099   .4766498     0.05   0.956    -.9163323    .9687521
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
gpa1.phat = FixedEffectModels.predict(m89, gpa1)
gpa1.h    = gpa1.phat .* (1 .- gpa1.phat)
gpa1.wh   = 1.0 ./ gpa1.h
m89w = stata_regress(gpa1, :PC, [:hsGPA, :ACT, :parcoll]; weights = :wh)
```

The feasible-GLS weights `1/[ŷ(1−ŷ)]` from the linear-probability fit are all
positive here (`min ĥ ≈ 0.141`), so the weighted regression is well defined and
reproduces Stata's `[aw=1/h]` estimates and standard errors exactly.
